posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
I’ve recently had several red Burgs from good producers that were over-the-top with high alcohol (14%) and jammy fruit.

Now, I am no lover of austere, cerebral wines. I like me some fruit in the glass, but these were too much-no particular structure, vintage or “place-ness” detectable. (Talkin’ to you, 16’ and 18’ Mugnier Clos de Marechale, 18’ Le Moine Chambolle 1er Baudes, 19’ Sigaut Morey 1er Millandes) To paraphrase a cogent CellarTracker comment “tastes like a hot mess of Merlot-dominant something-or-other.” Some of this is vintage, and some is the hand of the winemaker sure, but still…

My question is this: “Is there any reasonable hope that with time, these jam bombs are going to dry out/grow up, shed some baby fat and reveal some tannin/acid or at least become tasty/recognizable as Burgundy?” Is this a stage that big-ass young Burgs wines go through?

I know that with time puckering tannins will mellow, acid will balance fruit, leather and earth can emerge but…from jam? From something apparently lacking in balancing acid and tannin?

If this is what climate change bodes for Burgundy, it ain’t good.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: I can take those Marechales off your hands at an attractive price.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
Don’t know about these particular wines, but the first bottle of 1985 Clos de la Bousse d’Or i drank i found disgusting and tasting of red jujubes and tutti-fruiti. The wine was probably about four years old.

Bottles consumed much later were much better and I loved them.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Yep, plenty of hope but you have to wait for 10-20 years. Many people complained that the ‘09’s were jammy. Now they’re jammin’. And I hope you like jammin’, too.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I have had some wonderful 2003s over the past few years. If that vintage can settle down, any vintage can. I have not had any 2018s, but think that the 2016s I have tasted (not Mugnier) can be quite nice.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Jeremy beat me to it. 2009 took a lot of criticism early on for being too ripe and fruity from many burg lovers, now the wines are fantastic and still on an upward trajectory. I reckon a lot of 209s will be amazing from 2029 onwards.

And as Howard says if 2003s can come around so there are plenty of examples in history to be optimistic the 2018s will do the same.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked: Thank you Brodie- I actually loved that 16’ Mugnier initially, but now 3 years in it is getting thick. Hopefully time will help it.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked: Glad to hear it. Thank you all- I loved the 16’ Mugnier on release, but now 3 years in it is getting thick. Hopefully time will help it. I am a fan of Le Moine style and found 19’ to be a decent veitage, so was surprised by that Sigaut.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Thank you Brodie- I actually loved that 16’ Mugnier initially, but now 3 years in it is getting thick. Hopefully time will help it.

For me, I have found that the keys in young Burgs are (1) clean pure fruit, (2) long finish and (3) good acidity. If you have all of those in a wine, my guess is the wine will age pretty well.

It is not unusual for Burgs to pick up weight a year or two after bottling. It has caused me to totally reassess vintages like 1980, 1991, 2000, 2001, 2007 and 2017, which I initially found to be too light and thin.

For me, I like Mugnier wines but am not really a fan of Le Moine.

Steve,

First off, welcome to the forums. It looks like you have just crossed into double digits for your posts here. I have seen some wines settle a bit over time, but I’m talking 10-15+ years. So I’d suggest patience rather than trading or selling the wines off. For me, Burgundy is most often about having patience in the cellar. There are going to be some vintages that are tough, but like others have noted, I think there can be some beautiful wines with enough time in the cellar.

Anyway, yes there is hope for young, jammy Burgs. But they’re still not something I’ll go out of my way to seek out.

Agreed. I would say stick to your favorite producers in young, jammy Burgs. A lot of the reason I have had good 2003s is that the ones I have been drinking were mostly Truchots (the only producer I bought from the vintage) with a smathering of Mugneret-Gibourg.

I sometimes wonder if it would be best to go against type in buying producers for a vintage. For example, in a vintage like 2018, buy producers who make very structured wines (e.g., Chandon de Briailles) and in a vintage that is more structured like 2008 buy wines from producers that generally get richness in their wines. But, I have never really looked methodically as to whether this works out ultimately.

Howard, you have more experience with Burgs than I do. I’m wondering what your thoughts are on 2014 vs. 2017? I think these are the two I prefer the most in the 2013-2017 five year span. I think the 2015’s will take a bit more time as they seem more coiled and unyielding, but I’d like to hear your thoughts on the differences between 2014 & 2017 from your perspective if you’ve got the time.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
It’s odd to see people respond to a question about four specific wines from three separate vintages with things like “yes - 2003 and 2009”. My hunch is that the mugnier will turn out fine, and that the ‘16 is likely to be great, although I haven’t opened an ‘18 yet. I don’t have enough experience with the other producers to form a view on their wines, but hold onto the mugnier. It’s worth it.

As for the vintages, 2003 is to me the most inconsistent, even across producers. The wines that are bad are really bad, and some are not just roasted, but almost roasted and green at the same time, like a steak that got seared on the outside and left raw in the middle. It’s as if the sun cooked the skin before the rest was ripe. I’m sure some have turned out fine, but I wouldn’t seek them out and wouldn’t buy them expecting much without checking notes.

I’ve always found ‘09s to generally lack a bit of acidity. There are some good wines, but I still don’t think it’s met the criteria of a truly great vintage. The one after was better.

‘15 seems pretty promising to me, as does ‘19. ‘20 looks to be exceptional. I’m not sure about ‘16 and ‘18 to me as the seem like stylistic outliers, but they definitely don’t strike me as dire the way the ‘03s did on release and I’m happy enough to own both vintages.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I’ve had a 16 Mugnier Marechale and didn’t find it jammy. I have no concerns about 2016 in general, which I think is the best red vintage since 2010 and the second best this century.
I’ve not had the other wines. From tasting 18s and 19s, I’ve generally found 2018 riper than I like (and one winemaker compared it directly to 2003) and I think 2019 is good but heterogeneous. It is also by no means lean - there will be ripe wines. Certainly drinking 1er crus this early may contribute to the sense of ripeness - they’re likely to have more oak and extraction this young. The village 19s I’ve had from producers I usually buy haven’t exhibited this issue.

As far as the comparison to 2003…that would be bad, certainly, but I don’t think 2018 is that bad, even if I’m not a big fan.
Also, I agree there are some good wines from 2003 (as there are in any vintage), but that argument reminds me of the “don’t count out 2004 because LaTache is good” view. Forest vs trees.

I have not had or bought any 2018s. I like 2017 a good bit. I think it is comparable to 2001.

Howard, you have more experience with Burgs than I do. I’m wondering what your thoughts are on 2014 vs. 2017? I think these are the two I prefer the most in the 2013-2017 five year span. I think the 2015’s will take a bit more time as they seem more coiled and unyielding, but I’d like to hear your thoughts on the differences between 2014 & 2017 from your perspective if you’ve got the time.

Not sure that the reds ultimately will be that much different. I have a gut feeling that the 2007s will be a tiny bit better, but I could be wrong. I think every vintage from 2014-2017 will be quite good. I have more 2004s than 2007s because the 2004s were cheaper and because as I have gotten older I have cut back on wine buying.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As far as the comparison to 2003…that would be bad, certainly, but I don’t think 2018 is that bad, even if I’m not a big fan.

Exactly! Just in terms of chemistry, none of the big trio of '18, '19 or '20 are really comparable to 2003, where acidities were almost non-existent and pHs very high. In 2018 and 2019, pHs are above average but not extremely high, and in 2020 pHs are actually quite low.

Beyond any considerations of chemistry, suggestive though those are, the best growers are doing a better job of protecting fruit and vines from heat and sunburn (hedging less/higher, not systematically deleafing, working the soils more intelligently), harvesting in a timely fashion, and keeping fruit cool before it is processed. They have a lot more experience of vinifying high sugar musts, and of maturing higher pH wines.

All of this makes me very optimistic about the likely evolution of these wines vis a vis the 2003s (and indeed the more extreme 1990s, which can be a bit roasted in profile these days).

As others have observed, the extremely positive evolution of many red 2009s, which today often taste younger than their 2010 counterparts, is also cause for optimism.

While Burgundy is perhaps uniquely good at producing interesting, high quality wines in years of marginal maturity (as the best 2021s are going to remind us), ripe grapes remain the leading desideratum for producing great wine.

As for the Mugnier 2016 Maréchale, it’s an atypically concentrated wine due to yields being heavily reduced by frost that year. It sounds as if it hasn’t shut down. But 2016 was far from being a sunny, high-ripeness vintage, so aside from its unusual mid-palate density, it’s hard to reconcile its inclusion in a list of jammy, over-ripe Burgundies. The 2018 is also a somewhat atypical wine, having being hailed twice, and it is indeed rather chewy and unusually high octane; but in those respects it isn’t representative of what Mugnier produced in 2018.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
Thank you William, that is some excellent and detailed information.

I too was surprised to find that Mugnier 16’ tasting so heavy last week, when it was among my favorite 16’s tasted a couple years ago. I think 16’ was a good vintage that was overshadowed by 15’ and I am glad to have bought my 16s.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
This is an old thread but what’s the consensus on the original question? It really depends on what one means by “jammy”?

I’ve had a few wines that were atypically ripe from warm years, and they were well made wine with good acidity but aren’t my thing. Recent encounters include des Croix Corton 2015 and CLF Vougeot 2009. Reading the critics & CT notes on des Croix, I do see mentions on strawberry jam or cherry liqueur, which aligned with my tasting note this year, so I guess the wine has been like that for about 10 years since the release.

I get an impression that this style of wine doesn’t age much over 10 or 20 years (just keep being jammy/primary for a really long time), but I never tracked evolution of ripe red burgundy over many years so I don’t know. Do they even gain sous bois/mushroom type of tertiary aromas at some point?

These just seem like dark, dense beasts. Maybe they’ll become enjoyable with a lot of time but I stopped buying them after sampling a few.

posts.cooked:

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It’s easier to avoid the jammy ones. I would say I was burnt by 1990, but as I was able to sell most of them for a profit, the fact that I began to dislike the vintage didn’t really matter.

I have found generally I prefer less ripe vintages such as 2008 over 2009 and have bought accordingly. A couple of people mentioned problems with 2016, a vintage I have absolutely loved. Haven’t opened many Cote de Nuits, but all the Cote de Beaune have been exactly what I look for in great Burgundy. Time to pull out one of my Trapets though, and see if it is as good as I remember,

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
Peter_Chiu:

From my personal experience, Des Croix are always delicious specially the entry level and they never taste jammy to me…my personal preference.

Good to know. My experience is only this single bottle, so I probably shouldn’t judge the producer style based off of that. I need to try one from another vintage, perhaps from a cooler year.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
So what is the definition of jammy. Interesting question.

Given the recent climate change which meas the growing conditions in Burgundy is getting warmer and warmer.

Here is what I have been doing - for pure enjoyment of red Burundian wines :

I am a fun of Des Corix red and I have full confidence in David Croix as he is my preferred producer around Cotton Hill and i am very sure he will adjust the weather conditions of the growing seasons.

I always get multiple bottles of the same wine from all the vintages since 2014.

I often do many match-tasting of the same wine from 2 difference vintage - for example 2017 vs 2019 Les CVs; and/or 2014 Les Bressandes vs 2015.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
I’m not sure if my comment above may have sounded to negate your experience, but that wasn’t my intention, if you took that way. And I’m not experienced with the producer, so my comment is only for the specific bottle I had, not about the producer.

Peter_Chiu:

So what is the definition of jammy. Interesting question.

That said, when I used the word “jammy” here, I was speaking purely of its aromatics, and I’m not talking about anything on the palate - the wine was well made, balanced and had high acidity. What smells jammy to one has to be somewhat subjective in nature, but the specific wine I mentioned here is La Vigne au Saint bottling from 2015, though I’m not sure if you had this specific wine.

This is an excerpt from NM’s tasting note in 2016:

…showing just a touch of reduction on the nose, though underneath lie succulent strawberry jam, raspberry preserve and fresh orange zest aromas.

So I assume this isn’t likely just my perception. You can check cellar tracker to find someone’s tasting note that mentions “cherry liqueur” too.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Thanks for your explanations and no negative comments at all.

Opinions and TNs on red burgundy are very subjective. Thanks…

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
For me, jammy is just a personal and subjective descriptor for what tastes like a heavy, over ripe, low-acid “flabby” wine, especially Pinot. I didn’t know it had more standardized connotations to be honest. In general I have tasted enough 18 red (and white) Burgs that fit my personal definition of “jammy” that I mostly don’t buy 18s.

That said, I have thrown the dice on a few bottles from trusted producers/upper crus, assuming that these plots get the prime picking dates and the most particular attention in winemaking (Dujac, Hudelot-Noellat, Tremblay, H. Lignier, Drouhin ). Even then I feel a little cautious. I liked Fourrier’s 18 Gevrey CSJ quite a lot, just to name one, but it’s no shy flower.

I’d be “18-curious” to sip something from producers/areas I mostly associate with a leaner style (talkin’ to you Volnay, D’Angerville, Montille).

Louis Boillot’s 18 Volnay 1er les Angles for example is an easy glass of pleasure, though blind, I’d be hard pressed to call out “Volnay”.

@Mark_Golodetz such is the mesmeric power of your suggestion that I very nearly tapped a 16 Trapet Chambertin last night, a big favorite. Opted instead for a lesser bottle so as to save those tasty 16s to evolve and share.

fancy_title: Any hope for young, jammy Burgs?
title: Any hope for young, jammy Burgs?
